## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

  2. **Marshal zero, commit** — `tt/rbw-MZ.MarshalZeroes.sh`; commit the result with the marshal-zero signature in the message (signature shape designed in BBAAB)

  - Canonical infrastructure setup phase (BBAAC)

  - Multi-operator workflow (single-operator scope by design)

1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BB
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Release qualification gap surfaced by the ₣A_ depot-regen heat. Every existing test tier (`rbw-tf` fast, `rbw-tr` release) tolerates accumulated depot state, so silent first-build assumptions slip through. Recent spooks — kludge-aware-charge-prereq (k-prefixed hallmarks at GAR), ZRBOB_PROJECT (compose project name without runtime prefix), rbob_charged_predicate prefix omission, reliquary integrity-broken on negative test — all share a property: they only manifest on first-build paths, and none were caught until live verification surfaced them.
6	
7	This heat constructs `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine — a third tier that **refuses to run** unless marshal-zero state was just committed. The refusal is enforced by the test itself, not by ceremony or operator discipline.
8	
9	## Design decisions
10	
11	### Single operator prerequisite chain
12	
13	Operator has exactly one prerequisite chain: confirm Payor health → marshal-zero → commit → run `rbw-tP`. Payor OAuth is the only credential the operator must have ready; everything else (governor, retriever, director SAs and their RBRA files) is **minted by the qualification itself**, not restored from backup.
14	
15	### Entry contract — load-bearing
16	
17	`rbw-tP` fails-fast unless ALL of the following hold:
18	- Working tree clean
19	- HEAD commit is a marshal-zero commit (detectable signature baked into `rblm_zero` per BBAAB)
20	- RBRR fields are blank (prefixes, depot project ID, etc.)
21	- No RBRA credential files present (governor, retriever, director — all deleted by marshal-zero, recreated by the qualification)
22	- No hallmark fields populated in nameplates
23	- No depot-scoped fields populated in vessel rbrv.env files
24	
25	Operator cannot skip the prerequisite. This is the property that makes the tier catch the silent-first-build bug class by construction.
26	
27	### Pristine-lifecycle fixture architecture
28	
29	The marshal-zero gate AND the SA/depot lifecycle exercises live as cases inside one new theurge fixture (`pristine-lifecycle`):
30	
31	| # | Case | Idempotent? | Cost |
32	|---|------|-------------|------|
33	| 1 | `marshal-zero-attestation` | N/A — gate | Free |
34	| 2 | `depot-lifecycle` | Yes (throwaway-named) | One GCP project per run (soft-delete graveyard accepted) |
35	| 3 | `governor-lifecycle` | Yes (throwaway-named) | Free |
36	| 4 | `retriever-lifecycle` | Yes (throwaway governor + retriever) | Free + light read probe |
37	| 5 | `director-lifecycle` | Yes (throwaway governor + director) | Light Cloud Build / GAR write probe |
38	
39	Cases 4 and 5 each create their own throwaway governor (single-case independence is load-bearing); after the case, both throwaway SAs are forfeited. Cases 2-5 are idempotent against existing canonical state — they can run while a canonical depot/SA chain exists, against any state.
40	
41	**Single-case run** (e.g., `tt/rbtd-s.SingleCase.pristine-lifecycle.sh depot-lifecycle`) skips case 1's gate and exercises just the named case. **Full-fixture run** goes through case 1 first; case 1 gates the rest.
42	
43	### Canonical infrastructure setup is a separate phase
44	
45	After the pristine-lifecycle fixture passes, `rbw-tP` proceeds to canonical infrastructure setup (BBAAC): mantle real governor + deploy real RBRA, charter real retriever + deploy RBRA, knight real director + deploy RBRA, levy canonical depot. This persistent state is what reliquary inscribe, hallmark builds, and crucible runs depend on.
46	
47	Two depots created per pristine run: one throwaway (case 2 of fixture) + one canonical (BBAAC). Both quota-bearing.
48	
49	### Failure mode contract
50	
51	Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.
52	
53	### Tier layering
54	
55	Three tiers, escalating cost:
56	
57	| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
58	|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
59	| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
60	| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
61	| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |
62	
63	`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.
64	
65	### Operator scope
66	
67	Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. Runbook lives in README.md release section (5 human steps).
68	
69	### Coupling to ₣A_
70	
71	This heat runs concurrently with ₣A_'s remaining paces. ₣A_'s AAE+AAF+AAG sequence is the one-time cutover that informs `rbw-tP`'s sequence; pristine-tier construction proceeds in parallel and codifies the lessons. BBAAG's first end-to-end run is the natural validation point for both heats together.
72	
73	## References
74	
75	- ₣A_ rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen — surfaced the gap; AAE+AAF+AAG one-time burn-in informs but doesn't codify
76	- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — RBLM Lifecycle Marshal; `rbw-MZ` zeroes local regime, deletes all RBRAs, blanks hallmark/depot-scoped vessel fields. Marshal-zero signature is baked here per BBAAB.

78	- `Tools/rbk/rbh0/rbhocd_credential_director.sh` / `rbhocr_credential_retriever.sh` — handbook tracks describing director "build access" and retriever "pull/tally access" — operations the lifecycle cases 4 and 5 must verify
79	- `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — upstream contribution ceremony; pristine-pass becomes a precondition (BBAAH)

## Context

Every existing test tier (`rbw-tf` fast, `rbw-tr` release) tolerates accumulated depot state, so silent first-build assumptions slip through. Recent spooks — kludge-aware-charge-prereq (k-prefixed hallmarks at GAR), ZRBOB_PROJECT (compose project name without runtime prefix), rbob_charged_predicate prefix omission, reliquary integrity-broken on negative test — all share a property: they only manifest on first-build paths, and none were caught until live verification surfaced them.

This heat constructs `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine — a third tier that **refuses to run** unless marshal-zero state was just committed. The refusal is enforced by the test itself, not by ceremony or operator discipline.

## Design decisions

### Single operator prerequisite chain

Operator has exactly one prerequisite chain: confirm Payor health → marshal-zero → commit → run `rbw-tP`. Payor OAuth is the only credential the operator must have ready; everything else (governor, retriever, director SAs and their RBRA files) is **minted by the qualification itself**, not restored from backup.

### Entry contract — load-bearing

`rbw-tP` fails-fast unless ALL of the following hold:
- Working tree clean
- HEAD commit is a marshal-zero commit (detectable signature baked into `rblm_zero` per BBAAB)
- RBRR fields are blank (prefixes, depot project ID, etc.)
- No RBRA credential files present (governor, retriever, director — all deleted by marshal-zero, recreated by the qualification)
- No hallmark fields populated in nameplates
- No depot-scoped fields populated in vessel rbrv.env files

Operator cannot skip the prerequisite. This is the property that makes the tier catch the silent-first-build bug class by construction.

### Pristine-lifecycle fixture architecture

The marshal-zero gate AND the SA/depot lifecycle exercises live as cases inside one new theurge fixture (`pristine-lifecycle`):

| # | Case | Idempotent? | Cost |
|---|------|-------------|------|
| 1 | `marshal-zero-attestation` | N/A — gate | Free |
| 2 | `depot-lifecycle` | Yes (throwaway-named) | One GCP project per run (soft-delete graveyard accepted) |
| 3 | `governor-lifecycle` | Yes (throwaway-named) | Free |
| 4 | `retriever-lifecycle` | Yes (throwaway governor + retriever) | Free + light read probe |
| 5 | `director-lifecycle` | Yes (throwaway governor + director) | Light Cloud Build / GAR write probe |

Cases 4 and 5 each create their own throwaway governor (single-case independence is load-bearing); after the case, both throwaway SAs are forfeited. Cases 2-5 are idempotent against existing canonical state — they can run while a canonical depot/SA chain exists, against any state.

**Single-case run** (e.g., `tt/rbtd-s.SingleCase.pristine-lifecycle.sh depot-lifecycle`) skips case 1's gate and exercises just the named case. **Full-fixture run** goes through case 1 first; case 1 gates the rest.

### Canonical infrastructure setup is a separate phase

After the pristine-lifecycle fixture passes, `rbw-tP` proceeds to canonical infrastructure setup (BBAAC): mantle real governor + deploy real RBRA, charter real retriever + deploy RBRA, knight real director + deploy RBRA, levy canonical depot. This persistent state is what reliquary inscribe, hallmark builds, and crucible runs depend on.

Two depots created per pristine run: one throwaway (case 2 of fixture) + one canonical (BBAAC). Both quota-bearing.

### Failure mode contract

Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.

### Tier layering

Three tiers, escalating cost:

| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |

`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.

### Operator scope

Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. Runbook lives in README.md release section (5 human steps).

### Coupling to ₣A_

This heat runs concurrently with ₣A_'s remaining paces. ₣A_'s AAE+AAF+AAG sequence is the one-time cutover that informs `rbw-tP`'s sequence; pristine-tier construction proceeds in parallel and codifies the lessons. BBAAG's first end-to-end run is the natural validation point for both heats together.

1 # Recipe Bottle 2 3 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) provides two independent container image capabilities: 4 5 - **[Foundry](#Foundry)**: orchestrate Google Cloud Build to produce multiplatform container images (x86 + ARM), fetch, retain, and serve them using a role-managed private cloud registry — with optional [egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) builds and supply-chain [provenance](#Provenance) 6 - **[Crucible](#Crucible)**: run untrusted containers behind enforced network isolation — DNS filtering and IP filtering — even using images unmodified from "in the wild" 7 8 > [!IMPORTANT] 9 > **Early-stage project — security review welcome in both domains** 10 > 11 > The [Foundry's](#Foundry) egress-locked Cloud Build configuration — including the [SLSA](#Provenance) attestation chain, [build isolation](#BuildIsolation), and digest-pinned toolchains — has not yet had broad independent review. 12 > 13 > The [Crucible](#Crucible) runtime containment — a multi-container apparatus where the workload runs unprivileged in a network namespace it does not control — has also not had broad review, particularly the network isolation rules, privileged namespace setup, and egress enforcement. 14 > 15 > If you evaluate or deploy this, you are contributing to its hardening. 16 > Security-focused contributors and responsible disclosure are especially valued. 17 18 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is a set of bash scripts enabling enterprise grade container image management intended for incorporation into any project. 19 The dependency footprint is deliberately narrow — `bash 3.2` and a handful of standard tools — with no Python runtime, no language-specific package manager, and no `gcloud` CLI. 20 After initial manual setup, all cloud API calls use `openssl` + `curl`. 21 A small team can stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without specialized DevOps expertise. 22 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) goal is a workflow where every container image has a verified origin and a controlled version, running behind appropriate network safeguards. 23 24 **Project page**: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle 25 26

27 28

29 30 ## Environment 31 32 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is organized around two independent capabilities: the [Foundry](#Foundry) builds container images with verifiable [provenance](#Provenance), and the [Crucible](#Crucible) runs untrusted images with enforced network isolation. 33 The two compose but neither requires the other. 34 35 All configuration flows through [Regimes](#Regime) — structured `.env` files with typed validation, each with its own render and validate commands. 36 Some regimes are committed in the repo: [Vessel](#Vessel) definitions ([RBRV](#RBRV)), [Nameplate](#Nameplate) configurations ([RBRN](#RBRN)), [Depot](#Depot) identity ([RBRR](#RBRR)), and [Payor](#Payor) identity ([RBRP](#RBRP)). 37 Others live on the filesystem outside revision control: OAuth credentials ([RBRO](#RBRO)), role credentials ([RBRA](#RBRA)), and developer workstation paths ([BURS](#BURS)). 38 39 Every operation is launched through a [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) — a shell script in the `tt/` directory. 40 The critical property: tab completion finds the command you want. 41 Type `tt/rbw-` and the shell narrows to all [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) operations; type `tt/rbw-h` to see just the [Hallmark](#Hallmark) commands. 42 Each [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) is named `{colophon}.{frontispiece}.sh` — the colophon routes to the right module, the frontispiece tells you what it does. 43 44 Every state-changing [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) writes three [Log](#Log) files to the directory named by `BURS_LOG_DIR` in your [BURS](#BURS) station file: a stable-name file (always the same path — easy for tooling to locate and evaluate the most recent run), a per-command file (same command name across runs — tools like SlickEdit sense diffs between executions), and a timestamped historical file (permanent record). 45 Disk space is cheap; [Log](#Log) unconditionally so the diagnostic evidence is always there when something fails. 46 Handbooks don't [Log](#Log) — teaching output is ephemeral. 47 48 A [Transcript](#Transcript) is a single file capturing key decision points and state transitions within a [Tabtarget's](#Tabtarget) execution. 49 Where [Logs](#Log) preserve full terminal output, a [Transcript](#Transcript) records the structured progress of sophisticated orchestration commands — the first thing to read when debugging a multi-step failure. 50 51 The [Output](#Output) directory is a fixed-path staging area cleared and recreated before each [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) runs. 52 Commands that produce artifacts write them here. 53 Concurrent bash sessions share this path, so parallel commands can overwrite each other's [Output](#Output). 54 55 To begin, run the onboarding walkthrough: 56 57 ``` 58 tt/rbw-o.OnboardingStartHere.sh 59 ``` 60 61 ## Foundry 62 63 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) remote build orchestration system for producing, attesting, and distributing container images via Google Cloud Build and Google Artifact Registry. 64 The [Foundry](#Foundry) manages [Depot](#Depot) access, [Vessels](#Vessel) choreography, [Hallmark](#Hallmark) tracking, and build definitions. 65 Three [Vessel](#Vessel) modes determine how images enter the [Depot](#Depot): [Conjure](#Conjure) ([egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) build from source with [SLSA provenance](#Provenance)), [Bind](#Bind) (digest-pinned upstream mirror), and [Graft](#Graft) (local push). 66 Peer to [Crucible](#Crucible), which handles local runtime containment. 67 68 The [Foundry](#Foundry) orchestrates Google Cloud Build to produce container images with [SLSA](#Provenance) attestation, software bills of material, reproducible multi-architecture builds, and digest-pinned toolchains — so every image has a verifiable origin story. 69 Builds run in an [egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) configuration, drawing from upstream base images mirrored into a project-owned [Depot](#Depot) registry — a fixed, self-contained supply chain independent of third-party registry availability. 70 71 ### Depot 72 73 The facility where container images are built and stored — has its own GCP project with an artifact registry and a storage bucket, funded under the [Manor's](#Manor) billing account. 74 The [Payor](#Payor) [Levies](#Levy) a [Depot](#Depot), and the [Governor](#Governor) administers access to it. 75 Each [Depot](#Depot) operates as an independent supply-chain boundary with its own credentials, builds, and registry. 76 77 Each [Depot](#Depot) supports two build egress profiles: 78 79 - **[Tethered](#Tethered)** — Build egress mode allowing public internet access during Cloud Build. [Tethered](#Tethered) builds pull base images from upstream registries at build time — simpler to set up, but dependent on upstream availability. 80 - **[Airgap](#Airgap)** — Build egress mode with no public internet access during Cloud Build. 81 [Airgap](#Airgap) builds draw all dependencies from [Enshrined](#Enshrine) images in the [Depot's](#Depot) registry — fully self-contained, independent of upstream availability. 82 Requires [Enshrining](#Enshrine) base images before the first build. 83 See [Build Isolation](#BuildIsolation) for the security rationale behind these profiles. 84 85 ### Manor 86 87 The [Payor's](#Payor) administrative seat — holds the billing account, OAuth client, and operator identity. 88 [Depot](#Depot) projects are created and funded under the [Manor's](#Manor) authority. 89 The [Manor](#Manor) has its own GCP project, distinct from any [Depot](#Depot) project. 90 91 ### Payor 92 93 [Establishes](#Establish) a [Manor](#Manor) and funds [Depot](#Depot) projects through it; authenticates via OAuth. 94 The [Payor](#Payor) is the only role requiring manual Google Cloud Console interaction — [Establishing](#Establish) the [Manor](#Manor), configuring OAuth, and [Installing](#Install) credentials via browser flow. 95 All other roles descend from credentials the [Payor's](#Payor) infrastructure creates. 96 97 ### Governor 98 99 Administers a [Depot](#Depot): creates service accounts, manages access. 100 The [Governor](#Governor) is [Mantled](#Mantle) by the [Payor](#Payor) and holds the administrative credential for the [Depot](#Depot). 101 The [Governor](#Governor) [Knights](#Knight) [Directors](#Director) for build access, [Charters](#Charter) [Retrievers](#Retriever) for pull access, and [Forfeits](#Forfeit) credentials when they are no longer needed. 102 103 ### Director 104 105 Builds and publishes [Vessel](#Vessel) images into a [Depot](#Depot). 106 Each [Director](#Director) credential is scoped to one [Depot](#Depot). 107 The [Director](#Director) manages the image lifecycle: [Ordain](#Ordain) a [Hallmark](#Hallmark), [Tally](#Tally) registry health, [Rekon](#Rekon) raw tags, [Vouch](#Vouch) [provenance](#Provenance), [Abjure](#Abjure) superseded artifacts, and [Jettison](#Jettison) individual tags. 108 109 ### Retriever 110 111 Pulls and runs [Vessel](#Vessel) images from a [Depot](#Depot). 112 This is the most constrained role — read-only access to the [Depot](#Depot) registry. 113 The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images for local use, [Plumbs](#Plumb) their [provenance](#Provenance), or [Wrests](#Wrest) a specific image directly. 114 115 ### Vessel 116 117 A specification for a container image — built from source ([Conjure](#Conjure)), mirrored from upstream ([Bind](#Bind)), or pushed from local ([Graft](#Graft)). 118 Each [Vessel](#Vessel) is a directory under `rbev-vessels/` containing at minimum an `rbrv.env` configuration file; [Conjure](#Conjure) [Vessels](#Vessel) also include a Dockerfile. 119 A fourth mode, [Kludge](#Kludge), builds locally for development without involving the [Depot](#Depot). 120 121 ### Hallmark 122 123 A specific build instance of a [Vessel](#Vessel), identified by timestamp. 124 [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) are the unit of [provenance](#Provenance) tracking — each one records when and how the image was produced. 125 Each [Hallmark](#Hallmark) produces three tagged artifacts in the [Depot](#Depot) registry: the container image (`-image`), the [software bill of materials](#SBOM) ([`-about`](#About)), and the cryptographic attestation ([`-vouch`](#Vouch)). 126 [Hallmark](#Hallmark) values are recorded into [Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Regime](#Regime) files to pin a [Nameplate](#Nameplate) to specific image versions. 127 128 ### Foundry Lifecycle 129 130 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) uses a role-based security model with four roles, each building on the previous: 131 132 | Role | Authenticates via | Purpose | 133 |------|-------------------|---------| 134 | [**Payor**](#Payor) | OAuth (browser flow) | Creates/funds GCP infrastructure, manages [Governor](#Governor) lifecycle | 135 | [**Governor**](#Governor) | Service account credential | Administers [Director](#Director) and [Retriever](#Retriever) credentials within a [Depot](#Depot) | 136 | [**Director**](#Director) | Service account credential | Submits builds, manages images, verifies [provenance](#Provenance) | 137 | [**Retriever**](#Retriever) | Service account credential | Pulls images for local use | 138 139 The [Payor](#Payor) stands apart — it requires manual Google Cloud Console work and OAuth authentication. 140 All downstream roles authenticate via credential files, enabling full automation. 141 142 #### Establishment and Provisioning 143 144 The [Payor](#Payor) begins by [Establishing](#Establish) a GCP project and configuring an OAuth consent screen through the Google Cloud Console. 145 After downloading the OAuth client credentials, the [Payor](#Payor) [Installs](#Install) them via a browser authorization flow — the resulting refresh token is stored locally with restrictive permissions and does not need to be repeated. 146 147 With [Payor](#Payor) credentials in place, the [Payor](#Payor) [Levies](#Levy) a [Depot](#Depot), provisioning it with build infrastructure, artifact registry, and secrets storage. 148 The [Payor](#Payor) then [Mantles](#Mantle) a [Governor](#Governor) service account to administer the [Depot](#Depot). 149 150 Before the first build can run, the [Depot](#Depot) needs its supply-chain infrastructure in place: upstream base images must be [Enshrined](#Enshrine) into the registry, and a [Reliquary](#Reliquary) of builder tool images must be inscribed. 151 152 #### Credential Distribution 153 154 The [Governor](#Governor) creates downstream credentials: [Knighting](#Knight) a [Director](#Director) for build operations and [Chartering](#Charter) a [Retriever](#Retriever) for image pull access. 155 Each credential is scoped to a single role within a single [Depot](#Depot). 156 157 #### Build and Retrieve 158 159 The [Director](#Director) [Ordains](#Ordain) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) for each [Vessel](#Vessel) — [Conjuring](#Conjure) from source, [Binding](#Bind) from upstream, or [Grafting](#Graft) from local builds. 160 After builds complete, the [Director](#Director) [Tallies](#Tally) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) by health status and [Vouches](#Vouch) their [provenance](#Provenance). 161 [Hallmark](#Hallmark) values from the [Tally](#Tally) are recorded into [Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Regime](#Regime) files, completing the chain from build to runtime. 162 163 The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images locally for use. 164 165 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) builds container images on Google Cloud Build (GCB) and stores them in Google Artifact Registry (GAR): 166 167 - Isolated build environments using Google-curated Cloud Build builder images 168 - Multi-architecture support via `docker buildx` with binfmt emulation 169 - [SLSA provenance](#Provenance) attestation and verification 170 - [Software Bills of Material (SBOM)](#SBOM) for every build 171 - Full build transcripts captured as auxiliary metadata artifacts 172 - Upstream base images [Enshrined](#Enshrine) into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry, so builds do not depend on third-party registry availability at build time 173 - `gcloud` never runs on the workstation — REST calls via `curl` and `jq` drive all remote operations, and the Google-supplied `gcloud` binary is confined to Cloud Build step containers on the server side 174 175 Each build's source context is packaged as a [Pouch](#Pouch) — the security boundary between workstation and build infrastructure. 176 177 ## Crucible 178 179 The distinctive case [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) addresses is *running untrusted code*: third-party tooling, experimental packages, binaries with uncertain [provenance](#Provenance). 180 Containers excel at packaging known applications, but running unvetted code poses security risks that ordinary container deployment does not solve. 181 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) assembles a [Crucible](#Crucible) — three cooperating containers where a [Sentry](#Sentry) enforces network policy — without requiring modifications to existing container images. 182 The [Bottle](#Bottle) container runs unmodified, in a network namespace prepared by a privileged [Pentacle](#Pentacle), with all egress flowing through the [Sentry](#Sentry) gateway. 183 184 The [Sentry](#Sentry)/[Pentacle](#Pentacle)/[Bottle](#Bottle) triad running together as one unit defined by a [Nameplate](#Nameplate). 185 The [Crucible](#Crucible) is the local safety orchestration — the apparatus that makes running untrusted code practical. 186 [Charging](#Charge) starts all three containers; [Quenching](#Quench) stops and cleans them up. 187 188 ### Nameplate 189 190 Per-[Vessel](#Vessel) configuration tying a [Sentry](#Sentry) and [Bottle](#Bottle) together into a runnable [Crucible](#Crucible). 191 The [Nameplate](#Nameplate) moniker (e.g. `tadmor`) identifies the unit across all operations. 192 Each [Nameplate](#Nameplate) declares its [Vessel](#Vessel) selections, [Hallmark](#Hallmark) pins, and the network policy that the [Sentry](#Sentry) enforces. 193 194 ### Containers 195 196 - **[Sentry](#Sentry)** — Security container enforcing network policies via `iptables` and `dnsmasq`. 197 The [Sentry](#Sentry) applies two layers of egress policy: DNS-level filtering (only allowed domain names resolve) and IP-level filtering (only allowed CIDR ranges pass). 198 A compromised [Bottle](#Bottle) cannot bypass either layer — the [Sentry](#Sentry) is the sole gateway between the [Bottle](#Bottle) and the outside network. 199 - **[Pentacle](#Pentacle)** — Privileged container establishing the network namespace shared with the [Bottle](#Bottle). 200 The [Pentacle](#Pentacle) runs briefly with elevated privileges to create the network topology, then remains as the namespace anchor. 201 Security policies are enforced from the first packet because the [Sentry](#Sentry) configures the namespace before the [Bottle](#Bottle) starts. 202 - **[Bottle](#Bottle)** — Your workload container, running unmodified in a controlled network environment. 203 The [Bottle](#Bottle) has no direct network access — all traffic routes through the [Sentry](#Sentry) gateway in a namespace prepared by the [Pentacle](#Pentacle). 204 Any existing container image can run as a [Bottle](#Bottle) without modification. 205 206 ### Enclave 207 208 The isolated network connecting a [Bottle](#Bottle) to its [Sentry](#Sentry) — the [Bottle's](#Bottle) only path to the outside world. 209 All [Bottle](#Bottle) traffic routes through the [Enclave](#Enclave) to the [Sentry](#Sentry) gateway; the [Bottle](#Bottle) has no interface on any other network. 210 211 ### Crucible Lifecycle 212 213 [Charge](#Charge) the [Crucible](#Crucible) for a [Nameplate](#Nameplate) to start the [Sentry](#Sentry), [Pentacle](#Pentacle), and [Bottle](#Bottle) together — the [Bottle](#Bottle) is ready for interactive use immediately. 214 [Rack](#Rack) the [Bottle](#Bottle) to shell in, [Hail](#Hail) the [Sentry](#Sentry) to inspect the gateway, or [Scry](#Scry) the network to observe traffic across [Crucible](#Crucible) containers. 215 When finished, [Quench](#Quench) the [Crucible](#Crucible) to stop and clean up all three containers. 216 To inspect an image's supply chain, [Plumb](#Plumb) its [provenance](#Provenance) — the full view shows the [SBOM](#SBOM), build info, and Dockerfile; the compact view summarizes the attestation chain. 217 218 ### Reference Nameplates 219 220 Shipped [Nameplates](#Nameplate) demonstrating different [Crucible](#Crucible) configurations. 221 Each pairs a [Sentry](#Sentry) with a [Bottle](#Bottle) [Vessel](#Vessel) and defines the network policy for that deployment target. 222 223 **[ccyolo](#ccyolo)** — Claude Code sandbox for network-contained AI development. 224 The [ccyolo](#ccyolo) [Nameplate](#Nameplate) pairs the [Sentry](#Sentry) with a Claude Code [Bottle](#Bottle) under an Anthropic-only network allowlist — SSH entry from the workstation, OAuth authentication via copy/paste, everything else blocked. 225 [Kludge](#Kludge)-only development target: no cloud account, no service account credentials, fully self-contained on the developer's workstation. 226 The onboarding handbook's first hands-on track teaches the full [Crucible](#Crucible) lifecycle using [ccyolo](#ccyolo). 227 228 **[tadmor](#tadmor)** — Adversarial security testing [Nameplate](#Nameplate) for daily iteration. 229 The [tadmor](#tadmor) [Nameplate](#Nameplate) pairs the [Sentry](#Sentry) with the [Ifrit](#Ifrit) attack [Vessel](#Vessel) under a restrictive network allowlist, consuming [Kludged](#Kludge) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) for fast author-test-iterate cycles. 230 The [Theurge](#Theurge) test orchestrator [Charges](#Charge) [tadmor](#tadmor) and dispatches curated escape attempts to validate that the [Sentry's](#Sentry) containment holds under adversarial conditions. 231 232 **[moriah](#moriah)** — Adversarial security testing [Nameplate](#Nameplate) for the airgap supply chain. 233 The [moriah](#moriah) [Nameplate](#Nameplate) pairs the [Sentry](#Sentry) with the [Ifrit](#Ifrit) attack [Vessel](#Vessel) under the same restrictive network allowlist as [tadmor](#tadmor), consuming [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) [Ordained](#Ordain) end-to-end on the [Airgap](#Airgap) pool. 234 The [Theurge](#Theurge) runs the same escape attempts against [moriah](#moriah) as against [tadmor](#tadmor) — the cloud-built variant validating that containment holds identically when the supply chain produces the inputs. 235 236 ## Appendix: Foundry Operations 237 238 Formal definitions for all [Foundry](#Foundry) operations, organized by lifecycle phase. 239 240 ### Infrastructure 241 242 **[Establish](#Establish)** — Guided setup of a new [Manor](#Manor) — creates the [Manor's](#Manor) GCP project and configures the OAuth consent screen through the Google Cloud Console. 243 [Establishing](#Establish) walks the [Payor](#Payor) through project creation, API enablement, and consent screen configuration — the manual prerequisites before any automated operations can run. 244 245 **[Install](#Install)** — Ingest OAuth client credentials from a downloaded JSON key file. 246 [Installing](#Install) triggers a browser authorization flow and stores the resulting refresh token locally with restrictive permissions (`600`). 247 This is a one-time [Payor](#Payor) operation — the refresh token persists until explicitly revoked. 248 249 **[Levy](#Levy)** — Provision a new [Depot's](#Depot) GCP infrastructure. 250 [Levying](#Levy) creates the GCP project, artifact registry, storage bucket, and build configuration. 251 This is a [Payor](#Payor) operation that binds [Regime](#Regime) configuration to real cloud resources. 252 253 **[Unmake](#Unmake)** — Permanently destroy a [Depot's](#Depot) GCP infrastructure — project, artifact registry, storage bucket, and all contents. 254 [Unmaking](#Unmake) is the reverse of [Levying](#Levy). 255 This is a [Payor](#Payor) operation and is irreversible. 256 257 **[Refresh](#Refresh)** — Refresh an expired [Payor](#Payor) OAuth token. 258 OAuth tokens expire periodically; [Refreshing](#Refresh) re-authenticates via the stored refresh token without repeating the full [Install](#Install) flow. 259 Run when [Payor](#Payor) operations fail with authentication errors. 260 261 **[Quota](#Quota)** — Review Cloud Build capacity and usage. 262 [Quota](#Quota) displays the current build minute allocation, consumption, and any throttling in effect for the [Depot's](#Depot) GCP project. 263 264 ### Credentials 265 266 **[Mantle](#Mantle)** — Create or replace the [Governor](#Governor) service account for a [Depot](#Depot). 267 [Mantling](#Mantle) is a [Payor](#Payor) operation that provisions the administrative credential — the [Governor](#Governor) inherits the [Payor's](#Payor) authority to manage the [Depot](#Depot) but authenticates via service account key rather than OAuth. 268 269 **[Knight](#Knight)** — Create a [Director](#Director) service account. 270 [Knighting](#Knight) provisions a new credential scoped to build and publish access within a single [Depot](#Depot). 271 272 **[Charter](#Charter)** — Create a [Retriever](#Retriever) service account. 273 [Chartering](#Charter) provisions a new credential scoped to image pull access within a single [Depot](#Depot). 274 275 **[Forfeit](#Forfeit)** — Revoke a service account credential. 276 [Forfeiting](#Forfeit) deletes the service account and its key material from the [Depot](#Depot) — the credential becomes permanently unusable. 277 This is a [Governor](#Governor) operation used when a credential is compromised, no longer needed, or being rotated. 278 279 **[List Service Accounts](#ListSAs)** — Inventory all service accounts issued within a [Depot](#Depot). 280 Shows [Governor](#Governor), [Director](#Director), and [Retriever](#Retriever) credentials with their creation dates and status. 281 282 ### Supply Chain 283 284 **[Enshrine](#Enshrine)** — Mirror upstream base images into your [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 285 [Enshrining](#Enshrine) ensures the build pipeline has a fixed, self-contained supply chain — builds draw from project-owned copies rather than depending on third-party registry availability at build time. 286 287 **[Reliquary](#Reliquary)** — Co-versioned set of builder tool images (skopeo, docker, gcloud, syft) inscribed from upstream into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 288 Cloud Build jobs use [Reliquary](#Reliquary) images as step containers, ensuring builds run with known, project-owned toolchains rather than pulling tools from upstream at build time. 289 The [Director](#Director) inscribes a [Reliquary](#Reliquary) before any [Ordain](#Ordain) or [Enshrine](#Enshrine) operation can run. 290 291 ### Building 292 293 **[Ordain](#Ordain)** — Create a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) with full attestation — the production build operation. 294 [Ordaining](#Ordain) is mode-aware: it [Conjures](#Conjure), [Binds](#Bind), or [Grafts](#Graft) depending on the [Vessel's](#Vessel) configuration. 295 Each [Ordain](#Ordain) produces an image in the [Depot](#Depot) registry with associated [provenance](#Provenance) metadata. 296 297 **[Conjure](#Conjure)** — Cloud Build creates the image from source. 298 [Conjure](#Conjure) builds run in an [egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) environment with digest-pinned toolchains, producing full [SLSA](#Provenance) attestation and [SBOMs](#SBOM). 299 This is the highest-trust build mode. 300 301 **[Bind](#Bind)** — Mirror an upstream image pinned by digest. 302 [Binding](#Bind) captures an external image at a specific digest into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 303 Trust is established through digest-pin verification rather than build [provenance](#Provenance). 304 305 **[Graft](#Graft)** — Push a locally-built image to the [Depot](#Depot) registry. 306 [Grafting](#Graft) uploads a local image to GAR via docker push — no Cloud Build for the image itself, though [About](#About) and [Vouch](#Vouch) metadata still run in Cloud Build. 307 This is the lowest-trust mode (GRAFTED verdict). 308 309 **[Kludge](#Kludge)** — Build a [Vessel](#Vessel) image locally for fast iteration, without [Depot](#Depot) registry push. 310 [Kludging](#Kludge) produces a local Docker image for development and testing without involving Cloud Build or the [Depot](#Depot). 311 The resulting image can be used to [Charge](#Charge) a [Crucible](#Crucible) directly. 312 313 **[Pouch](#Pouch)** — Build context packaged as a FROM SCRATCH OCI image and pushed to the [Depot's](#Depot) registry before a Cloud Build job runs. 314 The [Director](#Director) controls what enters the [Pouch](#Pouch) — Dockerfile, context files, build scripts — and the cloud receives only what the [Pouch](#Pouch) contains. 315 This is the security boundary between workstation and build infrastructure. 316 317 ### Verification 318 319 **[Tally](#Tally)** — Inventory [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) in the [Depot](#Depot) registry by health status. 320 [Tallying](#Tally) shows which builds succeeded, which are pending, and which failed. 321 The [Director](#Director) [Tallies](#Tally) before [Vouching](#Vouch) to confirm build completion. 322 323 **[Rekon](#Rekon)** — Raw listing of image tags in the [Depot](#Depot) registry for a [Vessel](#Vessel) package. 324 [Rekon](#Rekon) is a [Director](#Director)-only diagnostic that shows exactly what exists in the registry without health interpretation. 325 Where [Tally](#Tally) groups [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) by status, [Rekon](#Rekon) shows the unprocessed tag inventory. 326 327 **[Vouch](#Vouch)** — Cryptographic attestation proving a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) was built by trusted infrastructure. 328 The [Vouch](#Vouch) verdict is mode-aware: [Conjure](#Conjure) builds receive full [SLSA provenance](#Provenance) verification, [Bind](#Bind) builds receive digest-pin verification, and [Graft](#Graft) builds receive a GRAFTED verdict with no [provenance](#Provenance) chain. 329 The [Director](#Director) [Vouches](#Vouch) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) after [Tallying](#Tally) their build status. 330 331 **[About](#About)** — Build metadata and [software bill of materials](#SBOM) for a [Hallmark](#Hallmark). 332 The [About](#About) artifact (`-about` tag) contains the [SBOM](#SBOM), build transcript, build configuration snapshot, and key package summaries — bundled as a compressed archive and stored as a Generic Artifact in GAR. 333 Every [Ordain](#Ordain) produces an [About](#About) alongside the image. 334 335 **[Plumb](#Plumb)** — Inspect an artifact's [provenance](#Provenance) — [SBOM](#SBOM), build info, and [Vouch](#Vouch) chain. 336 [Plumbing](#Plumb) provides full transparency into how an image was built and what it contains. 337 Two views are available: full ([SBOM](#SBOM), build info, Dockerfile) and compact (attestation summary). 338 339 ### Distribution 340 341 **[Summon](#Summon)** — Pull a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) image from the [Depot](#Depot) to your local machine. 342 The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images for local use — the final step before a [Hallmark's](#Hallmark) image can be used in a [Crucible](#Crucible). 343 344 **[Wrest](#Wrest)** — Pull a specific image from the [Depot](#Depot) registry by reference. 345 [Wresting](#Wrest) is a direct pull without [Vouch](#Vouch) verification — used when you need a specific image tag regardless of attestation status. 346 Compare with [Summon](#Summon), which enforces the [Vouch](#Vouch) ceremony. 347 348 ### Removal 349 350 **[Abjure](#Abjure)** — Remove a [Hallmark's](#Hallmark) artifacts from the [Depot's](#Depot) registry — the `-image`, [`-about`](#About), and [`-vouch`](#Vouch) tags deleted as a coherent unit. 351 [Abjuring](#Abjure) is the reverse of [Ordaining](#Ordain): it formally renounces a build instance. 352 The [Director](#Director) [Abjures](#Abjure) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) that are superseded, broken, or no longer needed. 353 354 **[Jettison](#Jettison)** — Delete a specific image tag from the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 355 [Jettisoning](#Jettison) is lower-level than [Abjure](#Abjure) — it removes a single tag rather than a complete [Hallmark](#Hallmark) artifact set. 356 Used for cleanup of individual registry entries. 357 358 ### Diagnostics 359 360 **[List Depots](#ListDepots)** — Inventory all active [Depots](#Depot) visible to the current [Payor](#Payor) credentials. 361 Shows project IDs, regions, and provisioning status. 362 363 **[JWT Probe](#JWTProbe)** — Test service account authentication. 364 The [JWT Probe](#JWTProbe) verifies that a [Governor](#Governor), [Director](#Director), or [Retriever](#Retriever) credential can successfully authenticate to the [Depot's](#Depot) GCP project — useful for diagnosing access failures after credential creation or rotation. 365 366 **[OAuth Probe](#OAuthProbe)** — Test [Payor](#Payor) OAuth authentication. 367 The [OAuth Probe](#OAuthProbe) verifies that the stored refresh token can obtain a valid access token — useful for diagnosing [Payor](#Payor) operation failures before attempting a full [Refresh](#Refresh). 368 369 ## Appendix: Crucible Operations 370 371 Formal definitions for all [Crucible](#Crucible) operations. 372 373 ### Lifecycle 374 375 **[Charge](#Charge)** — Start a [Crucible](#Crucible) — the [Sentry](#Sentry)/[Pentacle](#Pentacle)/[Bottle](#Bottle) triad — defined by a [Nameplate](#Nameplate). 376 [Charging](#Charge) brings up the [Crucible](#Crucible) in dependency order: [Pentacle](#Pentacle) creates the namespace, [Sentry](#Sentry) configures policy, then the [Bottle](#Bottle) starts with its network already constrained. 377 378 **[Quench](#Quench)** — Stop and clean up a [Charged](#Charge) [Crucible's](#Crucible) containers. 379 [Quenching](#Quench) tears down the [Crucible](#Crucible) in reverse order and removes the network resources created during [Charging](#Charge). 380 381 ### Interaction 382 383 **[Rack](#Rack)** — Shell into a [Bottle](#Bottle) container. 384 [Racking](#Rack) opens an interactive session inside the running workload — for debugging, inspecting state, or running commands as the [Bottle](#Bottle) user would experience them. 385 386 **[Hail](#Hail)** — Shell into a [Sentry](#Sentry) container. 387 [Hailing](#Hail) opens an interactive session on the gateway — for inspecting `iptables` rules, `dnsmasq` configuration, network state, and egress logs. 388 389 **[Scry](#Scry)** — Observe network traffic across [Crucible](#Crucible) containers. 390 [Scrying](#Scry) captures packets on the [Crucible's](#Crucible) network interfaces — for verifying that blocked traffic is actually blocked, diagnosing connectivity issues, or watching the [Sentry's](#Sentry) filtering in action. 391 392 ## Appendix: Adversarial Test Method 393 394 The [Crucible's](#Crucible) containment is validated through coordinated escape testing using two components: 395 396 - **[Ifrit](#Ifrit)** — Adversarial attack [Vessel](#Vessel) purpose-built to run inside a [Bottle](#Bottle), seeking escape. 397 The [Ifrit](#Ifrit) carries scapy (arbitrary packet construction), strace (syscall boundary probing), and a minimal footprint — tools chosen to probe every surface the [Sentry's](#Sentry) containment exposes. 398 Named for the djinn imprisoned in a bottle. 399 - **[Theurge](#Theurge)** — Test orchestrator running on the host, outside the [Crucible](#Crucible). 400 The [Theurge](#Theurge) [Charges](#Charge) a [Crucible](#Crucible) with the [Ifrit](#Ifrit) as its [Bottle](#Bottle), then dispatches curated, reproducible, version-controlled attack scripts targeting specific surfaces: DNS exfiltration, ICMP covert channels, cloud metadata probing, namespace breakout, and direct IP bypass attempts. 401 Each attack runs inside the [Bottle](#Bottle) while the [Theurge](#Theurge) simultaneously observes the [Sentry's](#Sentry) network from outside — confirming that blocked traffic is actually blocked, not merely unrequested. 402 403 The escape tests were developed through adversarial Claude Code sessions with full visibility into the [Sentry's](#Sentry) source, configuration, and the [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) specification. 404 The [Ifrit](#Ifrit) [Vessel](#Vessel) is the delivery vehicle; the intelligence came from the authoring process. 405 Every test that passes is evidence the containment holds — not proof. 406 The test suite grows as new attack surfaces are identified. 407 408 ## Appendix: Supply Chain Provenance 409 410 Supply chain provenance is a cryptographically signed record of how a container image was produced — what source, what builder, what steps — so that consumers can verify an image came from trusted infrastructure and was not tampered with in transit or at rest. 411 412 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) achieves [SLSA](https://slsa.dev) v1.0 Build Level 3 for [Conjure](#Conjure) builds, auto-generated by Google Cloud Build. 413 The [Vouch](#Vouch) step independently verifies each build's DSSE envelope signature against Google's attestor public keys from `projects/verified-builder` KMS — using Python standard library and `openssl` only, with no third-party verifier. 414 415 Provenance guarantees are mode-aware: 416 417 | [Vessel](#Vessel) Mode | Trust Basis | [Vouch](#Vouch) Verdict | 418 |------|-------------|------| 419 | [**Conjure**](#Conjure) | Full SLSA v1.0 Level 3 — signed build provenance from GCB | DSSE envelope signature verification | 420 | [**Bind**](#Bind) | Digest-pin comparison — image in GAR matches pinned upstream reference | Digest-pin match | 421 | [**Graft**](#Graft) | Locally built and pushed — no cloud build involvement | GRAFTED (explicit no-provenance marker) | 422 423 Deliberately excluded: no `slsa-verifier` binary, no `gcloud` CLI on the workstation, no `jq` in the verification path. 424 The [Vouch](#Vouch) verifier reconstructs Pre-Authenticated Encoding (PAE), decodes the base64url payload and signature, and verifies via `openssl dgst` against embedded attestor keys — a minimal, auditable trust chain. 425 426 ## Appendix: Software Bill of Materials 427 428 A Software Bill of Materials ([SBOM](#SBOM)) is a machine-readable inventory of every component inside a container image — every OS package, every library, every binary, with versions. 429 Without one, a container image is an opaque filesystem whose contents you discover by running it, which is exactly the wrong time to find out it ships a vulnerable dependency. 430 431 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) generates an [SBOM](#SBOM) for every build using [Syft](https://github.com/anchore/syft), scanning each per-platform image during the [About](#About) assembly step. 432 Each architecture gets its own [SBOM](#SBOM), bundled alongside the build transcript and configuration snapshot in the `-about` artifact stored as a Generic Artifact in GAR. 433 434 An [SBOM](#SBOM) enables three hygiene practices that opaque images cannot support: 435 436 - **CVE triage before deployment** — when a vulnerability is announced, search your [SBOMs](#SBOM) rather than scanning running containers 437 - **Pre-deployment audit** — know what you are granting network access to before a [Crucible](#Crucible) is [Charged](#Charge) 438 - **Build-over-build drift detection** — compare [SBOMs](#SBOM) across [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) to see what changed between builds 439 440 The [Plumb](#Plumb) command surfaces [SBOM](#SBOM) contents: the full view shows package inventories; the compact view summarizes key components. 441 442 ## Appendix: Build Isolation 443 444 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) supports two build egress profiles — [Tethered](#Tethered) and [Airgap](#Airgap) — that determine whether a Cloud Build job can reach the public internet. 445 The distinction is not primarily about availability; it is a security boundary that controls what can enter and exit the build environment. 446 447 **What [Airgap](#Airgap) protects: exfiltration and supply chain injection.** 448 If a compromised dependency, build plugin, or Dockerfile instruction executes during your build, an [Airgapped](#Airgap) build cannot phone home — it cannot transmit source code, secrets, or intermediate artifacts to an external endpoint, and it cannot silently fetch malicious payloads. 449 This is defense-in-depth for proprietary code: even if a build step is compromised, the network is not available as an exfiltration channel. 450 451 **The curated gate principle.** 452 [Airgap](#Airgap) does not mean "nothing external." It means all external content enters through a single auditable gate — the [Enshrine](#Enshrine) ceremony — rather than ad-hoc network fetches during build. 453 The attack surface collapses from "any URL a Dockerfile mentions" to "the specific digests the [Director](#Director) [Enshrined](#Enshrine)." 454 Builder tool images enter through a parallel gate: the [Reliquary](#Reliquary), inscribed once and pinned by digest for all subsequent builds. 455 456 **What [Airgap](#Airgap) does not protect: the base image contents.** 457 Base images like `debian-slim` were themselves built with full internet access — `apt-get install` already ran inside them. 458 The [Airgap](#Airgap) protects *your* build steps on top of those bases, not the base image contents themselves. 459 Base images are vetted separately: digest-pinned at [Enshrine](#Enshrine) time, inspectable via [SBOM](#SBOM), and stored as project-owned copies in the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 460 A [Tethered](#Tethered) build of the base image followed by an [Airgapped](#Airgap) build of your application is the expected pattern — the base image is a known input, your proprietary layers are the protected output. 461 462 **Regulatory alignment.** 463 No framework mandates build-time network blocking by name, but egress-locked builds are the simplest way to evidence several common controls: FedRAMP CM-7 (least functionality) and SC-7 (boundary protection), SOC 2 CC6.1 (logical access) and CC8.1 (change management), and SLSA Level 3's hermetic build requirement. 464 465 ## Appendix: Roadmap 466 467 The following features are not yet implemented but are under consideration: 468 469 - **Crucible conduit for cloud services** - Encrypted tunnel from the [Sentry](#Sentry) to a VPC hosting PrivateLink endpoints, enabling [Bottles](#Bottle) to reach cloud AI services (AWS Bedrock, Vertex AI, Azure OpenAI) without exposing floating cloud IP ranges in the CIDR allowlist. 470 WireGuard terminated at the [Sentry](#Sentry) replaces per-service IP tracking with a single stable VPC CIDR. 471 Near-term, allowlist-only [Nameplates](#Nameplate) targeting specific service CIDRs and domains work today with existing [Sentry](#Sentry) machinery. 472 The tunnel adds defense-in-depth for PrivateLink-capable services; SaaS endpoints without PrivateLink (GitHub.com, GitLab.com) remain served by CIDR/domain allowlisting. 473 474 - **Credential confinement** - Move cloud service credentials (API keys, IAM keys, SSH keys) from the operator's workstation into the [Crucible](#Crucible), injected via [Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Regime](#Regime) configuration. 475 The workstation [Charges](#Charge) the [Crucible](#Crucible) but never holds service credentials — reducing the attack surface from "everything on the workstation" to "one minimal container." 476 Naturally paired with conduit work: credentials and network access are configured together. 477 478 - **VPC Service Controls** - Google Cloud security perimeters that prevent data from being copied out of a project even if an attacker holds valid credentials. 479 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) Cloud Build architecture uses private pools, which are the prerequisite for VPC enforcement; enabling the controls themselves is deferred until organizational policy or external distribution requires them. 480 If a VPC is stood up for the crucible conduit architecture, the VPC-SC perimeter should serve both Cloud Build [egress lockdown](#BuildIsolation) and [Bottle](#Bottle) conduit consumers. 481 482 - **Cosign container signing** - Cryptographic image signatures independent of registry trust. 483 Deferred alongside VPC Service Controls until external distribution triggers the need. 484 485 - **CDN-aware IP gating** - When allowed domains are CDN-hosted (e.g. Cloudflare), the [Sentry's](#Sentry) CIDR allowlist becomes coarse: DNS-level gating remains precise, but IP-level gating is porous across shared CDN address ranges. 486 A tighter mechanism is recognized but not yet designed. 487 488 - **Podman support** - The spec accommodates Podman as an alternative container runtime, but support is deferred. 489 On macOS, both Docker and Podman run Linux containers inside a hidden Linux VM — there is no native container runtime on Darwin. 490 Podman support would require managing that VM's lifecycle within the customer's [Depot](#Depot), adding infrastructure complexity with no architectural advantage over Docker Desktop. 491 492 - **[Crucible](#Crucible)-to-[Crucible](#Crucible) networking** - Under the current [Sentry](#Sentry) model, [Bottles](#Bottle) have no direct network path to each other; any inter-[Bottle](#Bottle) communication would route through their respective [Sentries](#Sentry). 493 The plumbing is feasible but not implemented, pending a concrete use case. 494 495 ## Appendix: Reference Project 496 497 This repository is the reference implementation of [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle). 498 The annotated tree below maps its files to the concepts defined above. 499 500 | Path | Description | 501 |------|-------------| 502 | `Project Root/` | | 503 | `├── CLAUDE.md` | [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) command reference, glossary, conventions | 504 | `├── tt/` | 136 [Tabtargets](#Tabtarget) — `tt/rbw-` for all operations | 505 | `├── Tools/` | | 506 | `│ ├── buk/` | Bash Utility Kit — portable CLI infrastructure | 507 | `│ └── rbk/` | Recipe Bottle Kit — domain logic | 508 | `├── .buk/` | [BURC](#BURC) project structure [Regime](#Regime) | 509 | `├── .rbk/` | [Regime](#Regime) configuration root | 510 | `│ ��── rbrp.env` | [RBRP](#RBRP) — [Payor](#Payor) identity for this [Depot](#Depot) | 511 | `│ ├── rbrr.env` | [RBRR](#RBRR) — [Depot](#Depot) identity and build configuration | 512 | `│ ├── ccyolo/` | [Nameplate](#Nameplate) — [ccyolo](#ccyolo) Claude Code sandbox | 513 | `│ │ └── rbrn.env` | [RBRN](#RBRN) — [Sentry](#Sentry) + Claude Code, Anthropic-only allowlist | 514 | `│ ├── tadmor/` | [Nameplate](#Nameplate) — [tadmor](#tadmor) adversarial testing | 515 | `│ │ └── rbrn.env` | [RBRN](#RBRN) — [Sentry](#Sentry) + [Ifrit](#Ifrit), restrictive allowlist | 516 | `│ ├── moriah/` | [Nameplate](#Nameplate) — [moriah](#moriah) airgap-built adversarial testing | 517 | `│ │ └── rbrn.env` | [RBRN](#RBRN) — [Sentry](#Sentry) + [Ifrit](#Ifrit), restrictive allowlist | 518 | `│ ├── srjcl/` | [Nameplate](#Nameplate) — Jupyter notebook | 519 | `│ │ └── rbrn.env` | [RBRN](#RBRN) — [Sentry](#Sentry) + Jupyter, academic-domain allowlist | 520 | `│ └── pluml/` | [Nameplate](#Nameplate) — PlantUML diagram server | 521 | `│ └── rbrn.env` | [RBRN](#RBRN) — [Sentry](#Sentry) + PlantUML, no-egress allowlist | 522 | `└── rbev-vessels/` | [Vessel](#Vessel) definitions | 523 | ` ├── common-sentry-context/` | Shared [Sentry](#Sentry)/[Pentacle](#Pentacle) build context | 524 | ` │ ├── Dockerfile` | debian-slim + iptables + dnsmasq | 525 | ` │ ├── rbjs_sentry.sh` | [Sentry](#Sentry) runtime — policy engine | 526 | ` │ └── rbjp_pentacle.sh` | [Pentacle](#Pentacle) runtime — namespace setup | 527 | ` ├── rbev-sentry-deb-tether/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) — [Sentry](#Sentry) (tethered, upstream pull) | 528 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode, tether egress | 529 | ` ├── rbev-sentry-deb-airgap/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) — [Sentry](#Sentry) (airgapped, enshrined bases) | 530 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode, airgap egress | 531 | ` ├── rbev-bottle-ccyolo/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) — [ccyolo](#ccyolo) Claude Code sandbox | 532 | ` │ ├── Dockerfile` | node:22-slim + SSH + Claude Code | 533 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode | 534 | ` ├── common-ifrit-context/` | Shared [Ifrit](#Ifrit) build context (tether + airgap variants) | 535 | ` │ ├── Dockerfile.tether` | Rust binary + network attack tools (tether build) | 536 | ` │ ├── Dockerfile.airgap` | Same image built `FROM` the forge ([Airgap](#Airgap) build, no upstream pull) | 537 | ` │ ├── Cargo.toml` | Ifrit crate manifest | 538 | ` │ └── src/` | Ifrit source (rbid binary) | 539 | ` ├── common-ifrit-forge-context/` | Build context for the [Ifrit](#Ifrit) forge — warms cargo cache + pre-stages apt deps | 540 | ` │ └── Dockerfile` | Forge image; consumed by `Dockerfile.airgap` as its base | 541 | ` ├── rbev-bottle-ifrit-tether/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) (tether) — [Ifrit](#Ifrit) attack binary | 542 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode, tether egress | 543 | ` ├── rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) ([Airgap](#Airgap)) — [Ifrit](#Ifrit) attack binary, [Ordained](#Ordain) without upstream pull | 544 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode, airgap egress | 545 | ` ├── rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) (tether) — Build-time forge fixture for [Airgap](#Airgap) [Ifrit](#Ifrit) | 546 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode, tether egress | 547 | ` ├── rbev-bottle-plantuml/` | [Bind](#Bind) — upstream image pinned by digest | 548 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Bind](#Bind) mode, digest reference | 549 | ` ├── rbev-bottle-anthropic-jupyter/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) — Jupyter notebook server | 550 | ` │ ├── Dockerfile` | | 551 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode | 552 | ` └── (4 additional test vessels)` | busybox variants for [Theurge](#Theurge) fixture coverage | 553 554 ## Appendix: Specific Regimes 555 556 **[BURC](#BURC)** — Project structure configuration, in the repo. 557 [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) directory, tools directory. 558 559 **[BURS](#BURS)** — Developer workstation configuration. 560 Not in git. 561 Log directory, station paths. 562 563 **[RBRR](#RBRR)** — [Depot](#Depot) identity and build configuration — populated during [Levy](#Levy), consumed by [Director](#Director) and [Retriever](#Retriever) operations. 564 565 **[RBRP](#RBRP)** — [Manor](#Manor) identity — billing account, OAuth client ID, operator email, and the [Manor's](#Manor) GCP project. 566 In the repo. 567 568 **[RBRO](#RBRO)** — [Payor](#Payor) OAuth credentials — client secret and refresh token. 569 Not in the repo. 570 571 **[RBRA](#RBRA)** — Role credentials resident on user workstation, enabling [Governor](#Governor), [Director](#Director), or [Retriever](#Retriever) operations. 572 One credential file per role per [Depot](#Depot). 573 574 **[RBRV](#RBRV)** — [Vessel](#Vessel) configuration specifying [Bind](#Bind), [Conjure](#Conjure), or [Graft](#Graft) mode for creating [Hallmarks](#Hallmark). 575 576 **[RBRN](#RBRN)** — Per-[Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Crucible](#Crucible) configuration mapping two [Vessels](#Vessel) — [Sentry](#Sentry) and [Bottle](#Bottle) — with runtime and [Hallmark](#Hallmark) assignments. 577 578
